ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 covers sago as a food ingredient; no dedicated FoodOn term exists for prepared sago pudding dessert. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers sago starch/grain; no specific prepared sago pudding code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229954005 refers to sago as a substance; no specific prepared sago dessert concept exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of sago pudding is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one small bowl or cup as commonly served in Singaporean dessert stalls and bubble tea shops.

nutrient_source.title: Sago, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 20067)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked sago (NDB 20067 / FDC ID 169726) used as the primary nutrient basis. Values represent plain cooked sago pearls in water. Singaporean dessert preparations may add sugar and coconut milk, which would increase energy, carbohydrate, fat, and sodium; values here reflect the plain cooked base. HPB Singapore Foodo does not carry a dedicated sago pudding entry; USDA SR Legacy is the highest-priority approved source available for this ingredient.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Sago pearls (cooked) have a reported GI of approximately 70 (high) based on Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database, which lists sago as a high-GI starch. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 32.85) / 100 ≈ 23.0. Carbohydrate per serving = 21.9 g/100g × 150 g = 32.85 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content in plain cooked sago
hypertension.guidance: Plain cooked sago contains approximately 1 mg sodium per 100 g and poses minimal concern for hypertension; however, Singaporean dessert preparations that add salted coconut milk or preserved ingredients may increase sodium intake and should be consumed in moderation by individuals managing blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI ≈ 70) and high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sago pearls have a high glycemic index of approximately 70 and a glycemic load of approximately 23 per standard 150 g serving, causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion sizes, pair sago with low-GI foods or protein, and monitor blood glucose response carefully.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat and cholesterol in plain cooked sago
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain cooked sago is virtually fat-free and cholesterol-free, posing low direct cardiovascular risk; however, dessert preparations incorporating full-fat coconut milk contribute saturated fat, and the high refined-carbohydrate content may adversely affect triglyceride levels if consumed frequently by individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sago pudding (西米露, Xi Mi Lu) is a popular Southeast Asian and Singaporean dessert made from sago pearls — small translucent spheres derived from the starch of the sago palm (Metroxylon sagu) or tapioca. The pearls are boiled in water until they become soft, gelatinous, and translucent, then typically served in a sweetened liquid base such as sugar syrup, coconut milk, or fruit juice. In Singapore, sago desserts are commonly found at hawker centres, dessert stalls, and bubble tea shops such as HeyTea. The texture is soft, chewy, and slightly slippery, with a mild, neutral flavour that readily absorbs the sweetness of the surrounding liquid. Nutritionally, plain cooked sago is almost entirely carbohydrate (approximately 21.9 g per 100 g), with negligible protein (0.06 g) and fat (0.02 g), making it a high-energy, low-nutrient-density food. The glycemic index is high (approximately 70), meaning it causes rapid blood glucose elevation, which is a significant concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content of plain sago is very low (approximately 1 mg per 100 g), making it suitable for hypertension management when consumed without added salt. Sago is naturally gluten-free and vegan, making it suitable for individuals with coeliac disease or plant-based dietary preferences. It is a culturally significant dessert across Singapore, Malaysia, Indonesia, and other parts of Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: Is sago the same as tapioca?
How many calories in sago pudding?
Is sago good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: No dedicated HPB Singapore Foodo entry exists for sago pudding as a prepared dessert. Nutrient values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy for plain cooked sago (FDC ID 169726), which is an approved secondary global source and represents the plain base without added sugar or coconut milk. GI value is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, which is an approved GI source. Confidence is medium rather than high because the nutrient values reflect plain cooked sago rather than the full Singaporean dessert preparation, and no Singapore-specific primary source entry was available.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent plain cooked sago pearls (water only, no added sugar or coconut milk) per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169726. Actual Singaporean dessert preparations will have higher energy, carbohydrate, sugar, fat, and sodium values depending on added ingredients. 2. protein_g (0.06) and fat_g (0.02) are not zero but are nutritionally negligible; they are reported as sourced values and are not zero-macro cases requiring special justification. 3. saturated_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed as negligible/trace per USDA SR Legacy for plain cooked sago; naturally zero for this plain water-cooked preparation. 4. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for plain cooked sago with no added sweetener; naturally zero for the plain base. 5. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy for this entry. 6. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy for this entry. 7. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy for this entry. 8. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy for this entry. 9. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy for this entry. 10. GI of 70 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); this is classified as high GI. 11. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type as no dedicated prepared sago pudding codes exist in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT. 12. This record was served by HeyTea at Marina Bay Sands on field survey data at SGD 1.20.
